title: Perspective and Authored Space
subtitle: Viewpoint, placement, and the authored space of signed writing
summary: Authored viewpoint, spatial relation, symbol placement, and passage orientation are part of the writing problem rather than secondary visual detail.
synopsis: Authored viewpoint, spatial relation, symbol placement, and passage orientation are part of the writing problem rather than secondary visual detail. This layer links plane-based writing to the shape of a sign, sign text layout, and the formal signbox model.
cover_image_alt: A two-dimensional signed-writing field with viewpoint guides, placement paths, relational arrows, and a hand composing within authored space.

slides.title: What The Writer Authors
slides.summary: If signed writing inhabits a plane, the next question is what the writer composes inside that plane.

image_prompts.concrete: Writing theory slide showing a SignWriting signbox with viewpoint, placement, relation, and composition highlighted
image_prompts.abstract: A two-dimensional written field becoming authored space

slides.speech: This paper follows the plane-based typology argument by asking a more concrete question: if signed writing inhabits a plane, what does the writer actually author there? The answer is viewpoint, placement, relation, and composition.
slides.notes: Make the opening simple. This is the bridge from typology to authored structure.

slides.title: Authored Viewpoint
slides.summary: SignWriting uses learned viewpoint conventions rather than uncontrolled visual depiction.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic slide showing front view, top view, body-centered space, and learned viewpoint conventions in SignWriting
image_prompts.abstract: A stable viewpoint frame organizing spatial writing

bullets: Front and top views are conventions
The goal is readable written relation
slides.speech: SignWriting does not ask writers to reproduce raw three-dimensional vision. It uses learned viewpoint conventions. Front view, top view, orientation, and placement are part of a disciplined writing practice.
slides.notes: This slide separates writing from free illustration.

slides.title: Placement As Writing
slides.summary: Symbol placement and relation are part of the written form, not decorative layout added after the fact.

image_prompts.concrete: SignWriting composition slide showing symbols arranged in a signbox, with relation and placement marked as written structure
image_prompts.abstract: Symbols becoming meaning through spatial relation

bullets: The written sign is not an unordered symbol list
slides.speech: The strongest claim is that placement belongs to the writing itself. A written sign is not an unordered bag of symbols. Symbol identity, position, relation, and grouped composition form the written object.
slides.notes: This is the core argument for the signbox bridge.

slides.title: Bridge To The Signbox
slides.summary: If authored space matters, the technical model must preserve composed spatial writing rather than treat it as disposable rendering detail.

image_prompts.concrete: Bridge slide moving from authored space to a Formal SignWriting signbox model with coordinates and symbols preserved
image_prompts.abstract: Authored space becoming a durable signbox

bullets: The signbox makes spatial commitments explicit
Formal SignWriting carries the technical next step
slides.speech: If viewpoint, placement, and relation are part of authored writing, then the technical model cannot treat them as rendering detail. That is why this paper prepares readers for the signbox and for passage layout.
slides.notes: Close toward Formal SignWriting without making this paper technical.

slides.title: Bounded Claim
slides.summary: Authored space means learned written viewpoint and placement, not raw visual depiction or full three-dimensional reconstruction.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic slide showing authored viewpoint and placement inside a SignWriting signbox, with boundaries separating writing convention from raw visual depiction and 3D reconstruction
image_prompts.abstract: A written plane with viewpoint and placement held inside disciplined boundaries

bullets: Placement must be preserved as writing
slides.speech: The claim is bounded. Authored space does not mean that SignWriting reproduces raw three-dimensional vision. It means that writers use learned viewpoint and placement conventions inside a written plane. Those conventions are part of the written object and therefore need to be preserved.
slides.notes: Use this to prevent readers from hearing the paper as pictorial realism. The claim is about written convention and authored relation.
